interactions.summary: Jiang concludes that modern fear of death makes everyone slave-like, then cautions that monotheism's triumph was a thousand-year fight carried by crusades, inquisitions, and religious wars.

After a brief audience acknowledgment, Jiang clarifies that by 'less sophisticated' he means modern people are less intuitive and less able to imagine layered realities than premodern readers of works like the Iliad.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Brief unclear audience response followed by Jiang answer

interactions.summary: He names capitalism, science, and the nation state as the three forces that narrow imagination: money becomes highest good, objectivity hides collective hallucination, and citizenship replaces plural community identities.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang lecture monologue with comprehension checks

speaker_notes.note: The very short 'That's right' likely follows an inaudible or clipped audience question; the question itself is not recoverable from the transcript.
Single-word acknowledgment is not a substantive public question.

claims.claim: Premodern people treated slavery as the worst evil, while modern people treat death as the worst evil because they believe death means nothingness.
Monotheism created the modern world through a long struggle, eventually triumphing only through crusades, inquisitions, and religious wars against those who rejected orthodoxy.
Premodern sophistication means the ability to imagine physical and spiritual realities layered together, as in the Iliad's human conflict and divine negotiation.
Capitalism narrows reality by making money the highest good and rendering love or care unreal when they cannot be measured or financially exploited.
Objectivity does not exist; Jiang previews a later claim that reality is a collective hallucination shaped by imagination rather than an independent object.
claims.temporal_scope: Forward-looking lecture claim on 2024-12-28 about next semester.

claims.claim: The nation state becomes God by making citizenship the primary identity and putting national identities into competition with each other.

signature_moments.moment: If death is the worst evil, fear of dying makes everyone a slave.
signature_moments.source_phrase: We are all slaves, every one of us
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It compresses the existential cost Jiang attaches to modern materialism.

signature_moments.moment: The Iliad lets human fights and divine negotiations coexist as layered realities rather than superstition.
signature_moments.source_phrase: all these other realities that exist on top of each other
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It illustrates what Jiang means by premodern sophistication.

signature_moments.moment: Science class feels like truth, but for Jiang it is a religion that blocks imagination of other realities.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a religion that... hinders you from imagining other realities
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is one of the lecture's sharpest attacks on modern authority.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The intuitive and imaginative capacity to inhabit multiple layered realities at once.
Jiang's previewed next-semester idea that the universe is what people collectively imagine it to be, not an objective thing existing on its own.
The modern state replacing plural community identity with exclusive citizenship and national competition.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang explicitly says the objectivity and collective-hallucination material will be developed next semester, so later lectures may update this compressed preview.

uncertainty_notes.note: Short segments around the closing exchange do not preserve the student's words, so no audience question is extracted.
